# Scenario 9: Version Ranges (What to Do When a Version Range Picks Something You Never Tested)

## What This Scenario Demonstrates
This scenario demonstrates dynamic version resolution using ranges (e.g., `[1.0, 2.0)`).

## Dependency Graph
```{mermaid}
graph LR
    Root["Project Root"] --> LibA["lib-a:[1.0.0,2.0.0)"]
```

### Maven (pom.xml)

```{.xml include="/demos/maven-demo/scenario-9-ranges/pom.xml" snippet="s9-deps"}
```

## Expected Intuition
"Give me the latest version that is at least 1.0.0 but less than 2.0.0."

## Actual Resolution Results
*   **Maven/Gradle/SBT**: All will query the repository metadata to find available versions and pick the highest one matching the criteria.
*   In our demo repo, `1.0.0` and `2.0.0` exist. The range excludes `2.0.0`, so `1.0.0` is chosen.

## Classpath Reality
`lib-a-1.0.0.jar`.

## Why This Happens
The build tool performs a "select" operation against the available versions in the repo.

### What About Hard Version Ranges?
A version declared as `[1.0.0]` (with brackets) is a hard requirement. Unlike a plain `1.0.0`, which Maven treats as a soft preference that can be overridden by the "nearest-wins" rule, a bracketed version will cause the build to fail if a different version is selected anywhere in the dependency graph. This is Maven’s mechanism for expressing ‘this exact version must be used, or the build breaks.’ It is the strictest way to declare a dependency version.

## How Developers Commonly "Fix" This
Ranges are controversial because they make builds non-deterministic (a new release of `lib-a` can change your build without you touching it). Many teams ban them.

## Safer Ways to Take Control
Use Dependency Locking (Scenario 11) if you use ranges.

## Signals to Watch For
*   "It worked yesterday."
*   Builds failing on CI but passing locally (due to repo cache differences).

## How This Scales in Real Systems
Ranges are common in NPM/pip but less so in Java/Maven ecosystems due to the desire for reproducibility.

## Key Takeaway
Ranges trade reproducibility for freshness; use with caution (and locking).

## Related Scenarios
*   [Scenario 11: Locking](../scenario-11-locking/guide.qmd) - How to tame ranges.
